discoveryGroups.title: Key Collaborators
discoveryGroups.description: The producers, engineers, and fellow artists who shaped the Patti Smith Group's sound.

cards.title: John Cale
cards.context: Velvet Underground co-founder who produced Horses. His avant-garde sensibility gave the album its raw, unflinching sound.

cards.title: Lenny Kaye
cards.context: Smith's musical partner since 1971. Their first collaboration at St. Mark's Church launched the Patti Smith Group.

cards.title: Richard Sohl
cards.context: The classically trained pianist whose delicate playing was the perfect counterpoint to Smith's raw vocals. Died in 1990.

cards.title: Ivan Kral
cards.context: Czech-born multi-instrumentalist who played on Radio Ethiopia, Easter, and Wave. Later documented the punk era on film.

cards.title: Jay Dee Daugherty
cards.context: Has played drums with Patti Smith since 1975. The rhythmic backbone of the group for five decades.

cards.title: Tony Shanahan
cards.context: Smith's bassist in the current touring band. Has been her musical anchor since the mid-1990s return.

cards.title: Robert Mapplethorpe
cards.context: Took the iconic Horses cover photo — one of the greatest album covers in rock history. The image defined an era.

discoveryGroups.title: Movements & Scenes
discoveryGroups.description: The artistic movements and cultural scenes that shaped Patti Smith's world.

cards.title: CBGB & NYC Punk
cards.context: The Bowery club where Patti Smith, Television, Ramones, and Blondie forged a new sound. Smith's residency helped define the CBGB aesthetic.

cards.title: Beat Generation
cards.context: Allen Ginsberg, William S. Burroughs, and Gregory Corso — the literary rebels whose work became Smith's creative blueprint.

cards.title: French Symbolism
cards.context: Arthur Rimbaud and Charles Baudelaire — the visionary poets whose radical approach to language Smith channeled into rock and roll.

cards.title: Proto-Punk
cards.context: The Velvet Underground, The Stooges, and MC5 — the bands that broke rock's rules before punk had a name. Smith drew from all three.

cards.title: Downtown NYC Art Scene
cards.context: The Chelsea Hotel, Max's Kansas City, St. Mark's Poetry Project — the institutions where poetry, visual art, and music collided.

cards.title: Confessional Poetry
cards.context: From Sylvia Plath to Anne Sexton — the tradition of turning raw personal experience into art that Smith made electric.

discoveryGroups.title: Essential Music
discoveryGroups.description: The albums and songs that define Patti Smith's musical legacy.

cards.title: Horses
cards.context: The debut that merged poetry and punk. 'Jesus died for somebody's sins but not mine' — the opening line that changed rock and roll.

cards.title: Radio Ethiopia
cards.context: The raw, aggressive follow-up. The title track is 10 minutes of feedback-drenched free improvisation.

cards.title: Easter
cards.context: The commercial breakthrough, co-written with Bruce Springsteen. Proved punk poetry could reach the mainstream.

cards.title: Wave
cards.meta: 1979 · Dedicated to Pope John Paul I
cards.context: The farewell album before Smith's 16-year retreat from music. Features 'Frederick' and 'Dancing Barefoot.'

cards.title: Dream of Life
cards.context: The album that broke the silence, co-written with her husband. 'People Have the Power' became her anthem.

cards.title: Gone Again
cards.context: The return album, written in the aftermath of Fred 'Sonic' Smith's death. Raw grief transformed into grace.

cards.title: Banga
cards.context: Smith at 65, still pushing boundaries. 'Constantine's Dream' is a 12-minute spoken word epic channeling Dante.

cards.title: Hey Joe / Piss Factory
cards.context: The 7-inch that started everything. 'Piss Factory' is the autobiographical spoken-word B-side that proved Smith was unlike anyone else.
